name: Prusti — Auto-Active Rust Verifier via Viper
summary: ETH Zurich's Prusti is an auto-active deductive verifier for Rust that translates programs to Viper and uses Viper's permission-based reasoning + Z3. Derives the separation-logic permission framing automatically from Rust's borrow-checker output, so users write only functional pre/postconditions. Default panic-freedom mode verifies absence of overflows, OOB, unwrap panics with no annotations.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
ETH Zurich's **Prusti** is an auto-active deductive verifier for Rust that translates programs to Viper (intermediate verification language; see `data/verification-intermediate-languages` for the broader verification-IL family Viper belongs to) and uses Viper's permission-based reasoning + Z3.

**Ownership-to-separation lift**: derives the separation-logic permission framing automatically from Rust's borrow-checker output, so users write only functional pre/postconditions. **Pledges**: specification mechanism for reasoning about borrow-expirations (final values after the lifetime ends). **Default panic-freedom**: verifies absence of overflows / array OOB / unwrap panics with no annotations. Open-source, available as VSCode extension (Prusti Assistant); restricted to safe Rust subset; used in industrial pilots and academic teaching but no major production code base verified.

sections.title: Ownership-to-separation translation
sections.content:
Prusti's distinctive contribution is **automatically deriving the separation-logic permission frame from Rust's borrow checker**. In ordinary Viper verification, the user writes `acc(x.f, write)` to claim write permission to a field; in Prusti, the Rust compiler's existing borrow-checker analysis tells Prusti which references hold which permissions, and Prusti emits the corresponding Viper assertions automatically. The user writes only functional pre- and post-conditions in `#[requires]` / `#[ensures]` annotations.

**Pledges** (`#[after_expiry]`) handle the awkward case of mutable borrows: a function returning `&mut T` may transfer ownership to the caller, but the borrowed reference will eventually expire. Prusti's pledge mechanism lets the specification describe the final value of the borrow after expiry — a specification facility absent from Rust itself.

**Default panic-freedom mode** verifies absence of arithmetic overflow, array out-of-bounds access, and `unwrap`-on-`None`/`Err` panics with no user annotations. This is the entry point for many adopters: turn Prusti on, get "this function never panics" as a free side effect of the borrow-checker analysis.

sections.title: Viper backend and Z3 dispatch
sections.content:
Prusti compiles Rust MIR (mid-level IR) to Viper (Silver), the separation-logic VIL maintained at ETH Zurich. Viper's verification condition generator emits Z3 queries; Prusti's overall pipeline is Rust source → MIR → Viper → SMT.

The choice of Viper rather than Boogie or WhyML reflects Prusti's design priority: separation logic primitives (permission accounting, fractional ownership, predicate folding) belong in the IL itself, not the frontend encoding. This contrasts with **Creusot** (`creusot.json`), which compiles Rust to **WhyML** with a prophetic encoding of mutable borrows in a purely functional logic. The two tools target the same source language with very different proof models.

sections.title: Status and deployment
sections.content: Status (as of 2026-04): open-source under viperproject/prusti-dev, available as a VSCode extension (Prusti Assistant). Restricted to safe Rust today; unsafe Rust verification is the domain of RustBelt/RefinedRust. Used in **industrial pilots and academic teaching** but no major production codebase verified end-to-end with Prusti. Compared to Verus (which targets unsafe Rust and has Google/Amazon production deployments), Prusti's niche is teaching auto-active verification and verifying safe-Rust libraries with low annotation burden.
